explain.rule: The present simple is used to talk about: (1) habits and routines — things you do regularly, (2) facts and things that are always true, (3) opinions and feelings, (4) schedules and timetables. The verb stays in its base form for I / you / we / they. For he / she / it — add -s or -es to the verb. To make a question or negative, use the helper verb do (I/you/we/they) or does (he/she/it). When you use do/does, the main verb always returns to its base form — never add -s to the main verb in a question or negative.
explain.why_matters: The present simple is the foundation of English. You use it in almost every conversation — describing your job, your family, your daily life, your opinions, and universal facts. If you get this tense right, a huge part of everyday English falls into place. It is also the tense you will compare every other tense to, so understanding it deeply makes everything else easier.
explain.hebrew_note: Hebrew speakers make three very common mistakes here. First: questions. In Hebrew you form a yes/no question with intonation alone — 'אתה אוהב קפה?' In English you MUST use do/does — 'Do you like coffee?' You cannot rely on intonation in writing. Second: negatives. In Hebrew you say 'אני לא אוהב' — just 'לא' before the verb. In English you need don't or doesn't — 'I don't like'. Third: the third-person -s. In English, only he/she/it gets a change (-s), and it is easy to forget under pressure. Also important: do NOT use present continuous for habits. 'I am working every day' is wrong. 'I work every day' is correct.
explain.tip: A simple trick for the -s rule: point to the person. If you can replace them with 'he', 'she', or 'it' — the verb needs an -s. Everyone else — no change. And remember: as soon as you use does or doesn't, the main verb drops its -s completely. Does takes the -s so the main verb does not have to.

form.positive: I/You/We/They + base verb | He/She/It + base verb + s/es
form.negative: I/You/We/They + don't + base verb | He/She/It + doesn't + base verb
form.question: Do + I/you/we/they + base verb? | Does + he/she/it + base verb?
form.short_answer: Yes, I do. / No, I don't. | Yes, she does. / No, she doesn't.

spelling_rules.examples: teach→teaches, wash→washes, miss→misses, fix→fixes, go→goes, do→does

spelling_rules.rule: Verbs ending in consonant + y: change y to i, add -es
Verbs ending in vowel + y: just add -s

spelling_rules.examples: She has a dog. / He is a doctor. / It has three rooms.

frequency_adverbs.note: Frequency adverbs show how often something happens. They go BEFORE the main verb but AFTER the verb 'be'.

list.example: I always brush my teeth before bed.
We often eat together on Fridays.

good_examples.note: Simple fact about yourself — I, no change to verb
Habit — she gets -s on the verb: drink→drinks

good_examples.sentence: Do you speak any other languages?
good_examples.audio_text: Do you speak any other languages?
good_examples.note: Question with do — verb stays as base form: speak (not speaks)
Negative with doesn't — verb stays as base form: eat (not eats)

good_examples.sentence: The sun rises in the east.
good_examples.audio_text: The sun rises in the east.
good_examples.note: Always-true fact — the sun = it, so rise→rises

good_examples.sentence: We usually have dinner together at eight.
good_examples.audio_text: We usually have dinner together at eight.
good_examples.note: Routine — we, no change to verb. 'Usually' goes before the main verb.

good_examples.sentence: My mother teaches English at a high school.
good_examples.audio_text: My mother teaches English at a high school.

good_examples.sentence: Does your father work from home?
good_examples.audio_text: Does your father work from home?
good_examples.note: Question with does — work stays as base form, no -s

good_examples.sentence: The train to Jerusalem leaves at 9:15.
good_examples.audio_text: The train to Jerusalem leaves at nine fifteen.
good_examples.note: Timetable — present simple for fixed schedules

good_examples.sentence: I don't think that's a good idea.
good_examples.audio_text: I don't think that's a good idea.
good_examples.note: Opinion — think/believe/know/understand are used in present simple, not continuous

good_examples.sentence: Water boils at one hundred degrees Celsius.
good_examples.audio_text: Water boils at one hundred degrees Celsius.

good_examples.note: Frequency adverb 'never' before the main verb

bad_examples.reason: With she/he/it you must add -s to the verb. 'She work' sounds unfinished to a native speaker.
English yes/no questions need do or does. Intonation alone works in Hebrew but not in English writing or formal speech.
Don't is for I/you/we/they only. For he/she/it always use doesn't.
When you use does, the main verb drops its -s completely. Does already shows it is he/she/it.

bad_examples.wrong: I am working here every day.
bad_examples.reason: For routines and habits, use present simple — not present continuous. 'Every day' is a strong signal for present simple.

bad_examples.wrong: She always is tired on Mondays.
bad_examples.correct: She is always tired on Mondays.
bad_examples.reason: Frequency adverbs go AFTER the verb 'be'. With all other verbs they go before.

exercises.question: She ___ (work) in a bank.
exercises.hint: She = he/she/it → add -s to the verb
exercises.explanation: With she, he, or it we always add -s or -es to the verb in present simple. work → works.
exercises.hebrew_note: בעברית לא צריך לשנות את הפועל עבור גוף שלישי, אבל באנגלית חייבים להוסיף s

exercises.question: I ___ (not / like) spicy food.
exercises.hint: For I, you, we, they — use don't + base verb
exercises.explanation: Negatives with I/you/we/they use don't + base verb. Don't = do not. The main verb never changes.

exercises.question: ___ he ___ (live) near the city centre?
exercises.hint: Question with he → Does at the start, then base verb — no -s on the verb!
exercises.explanation: Questions with he/she/it: Does + subject + base verb. The verb loses its -s because Does already shows it is he/she/it.
exercises.hebrew_note: בעברית שאלה = טון בלבד. באנגלית חייבים does/do בתחילת השאלה

exercises.question: Water ___ (boil) at 100 degrees.
exercises.hint: Water = it → add -s. This is an always-true fact.
exercises.explanation: Facts that are always true use present simple. Water is 'it', so the verb gets -s. boil → boils.

exercises.question: They ___ (not / have) a car.
exercises.hint: They → use don't. The main verb stays in base form.
exercises.explanation: Negatives with they use don't + base verb. 'Have' stays as 'have' — never 'don't has'.

exercises.question: My sister ___ (teach) maths at a secondary school.
exercises.hint: teach ends in -ch → add -es, not just -s
exercises.explanation: Verbs ending in -ch, -sh, -ss, -x, -o add -es. teach → teaches, wash → washes, go → goes.

exercises.question: ___ you ___ (understand) what the teacher says?
exercises.hint: Question with you → Do at the start, base verb after subject
exercises.explanation: Questions with I/you/we/they use Do + subject + base verb. The verb never changes in a question.

exercises.question: He ___ (not / watch) TV — he prefers to read.
exercises.hint: He → doesn't + base verb. The verb loses its -s.
exercises.explanation: Negatives with he/she/it use doesn't + base verb. The verb stays in base form: doesn't watch, not doesn't watches.

exercises.question: The bus ___ (leave) every fifteen minutes.
exercises.hint: The bus = it. This is a timetable / schedule.
exercises.explanation: We use present simple for timetables and schedules. The bus = it, so leave → leaves.

exercises.question: David ___ (study) computer science at university.
exercises.hint: David = he. study ends in consonant + y → change y to i, add -es
exercises.explanation: study → studies. When a verb ends in consonant + y (study, try, carry), change y to i and add -es for he/she/it.

exercises.question: ___ your parents ___ (speak) any English?
exercises.hint: Your parents = they → use Do
exercises.explanation: Your parents = they. Questions with they use Do + subject + base verb. speak stays as speak.

exercises.question: She ___ (not / enjoy) long meetings at work.
exercises.hint: She → doesn't + base verb. enjoy ends in vowel + y so no spelling change.
exercises.explanation: Negative with she: doesn't + base verb. enjoy → enjoy (vowel + y, no change). doesn't enjoy.

exercises.question: He ___ (go) to the gym three times a week.
exercises.hint: go ends in -o → add -es for he/she/it
exercises.explanation: go is a special verb — for he/she/it it becomes goes (not gos). go → goes.

exercises.question: I ___ (always / drink) water before I go to bed.
exercises.hint: Frequency adverbs go BEFORE the main verb for all verbs except 'be'
exercises.explanation: 'Always' is a frequency adverb. It goes before the main verb: I always drink, not I drink always.

exercises.question: Choose the correct sentence:
exercises.hint: Think about which negative form is correct for she
exercises.explanation: With she/he/it, negatives use 'doesn't' + base verb. 'She don't like' is wrong — don't is only for I/you/we/they. 'Doesn't likes' is wrong — after doesn't the verb must be in base form.

exercises.question: Which question is correct?
exercises.answer: Do they live in Tel Aviv?
exercises.hint: They = I/you/we/they group → use Do
exercises.explanation: 'Do they live' is correct. Use Do for they. 'Does they' is wrong — Does is only for he/she/it. 'Do they lives' is wrong — in a question the verb stays in base form.
distractors: Does they live in Tel Aviv?
Do they lives in Tel Aviv?

exercises.question: My brother ___ football every Saturday morning.
exercises.hint: My brother = he → verb gets -s. play ends in vowel + y → just add -s
exercises.explanation: 'Plays' is correct. My brother = he, so we add -s. play ends in vowel (a) + y → just add -s → plays.

exercises.question: Does your mother ___ on Sundays?
exercises.hint: After does, what form does the verb take?
exercises.explanation: After does, the main verb always stays in base form: 'work', not 'works'. Does already signals he/she/it.

exercises.question: Which sentence uses present simple correctly?
exercises.answer: I always have breakfast before I leave the house.
exercises.hint: Which sentence describes a habit using the correct tense and word order?
exercises.explanation: Present simple is correct for routines. Frequency adverb 'always' goes before the main verb 'have'.
distractors: I am always having breakfast before I leave.
I have always breakfast before I leave.
I does always have breakfast before I leave.

exercises.question: The conference ___ at nine o'clock tomorrow morning.
exercises.hint: This is a fixed, scheduled event. The conference = it.
exercises.explanation: We use present simple for timetables and fixed schedules. The conference = it → starts.

exercises.question: She ___ tired on Monday mornings.
exercises.hint: With the verb 'be', the frequency adverb goes AFTER the verb, not before.
exercises.explanation: Frequency adverbs go before main verbs BUT after the verb 'be'. Correct: she is always tired. Wrong: she always is tired.

exercises.question: Find and fix the mistake: 'He don't understand the instructions.'
exercises.hint: Look at the negative helper verb — is it right for 'he'?
exercises.explanation: 'Don't' is for I/you/we/they. With he/she/it you must use 'doesn't'. The main verb stays as 'understand' — base form.
exercises.hebrew_note: don't לעומת doesn't — he/she/it תמיד מקבלים doesn't

exercises.question: Find and fix the mistake: 'Does she works in a hospital?'
exercises.answer: Does she work in a hospital?
exercises.hint: When you use Does, what happens to the main verb?
exercises.explanation: When you use Does in a question, the main verb returns to its base form. 'works' → 'work'. Does already carries the he/she/it information.

exercises.question: Find and fix the mistake: 'You like this restaurant?'
exercises.hint: How do we form a yes/no question in English?
exercises.explanation: Yes/no questions in English always need Do or Does at the start. Intonation alone is not enough in written English.
exercises.hebrew_note: בעברית שאלה יכולה להיווצר עם טון בלבד. באנגלית הכתובה חייבים Do/Does

exercises.question: Find and fix the mistake: 'My father go to work by car every day.'
exercises.answer: My father goes to work by car every day.
exercises.hint: My father = he. What happens to 'go' for he/she/it?
exercises.explanation: go is a special verb — for he/she/it it becomes goes (not gos). My father = he → goes.

exercises.question: Find and fix the mistake: 'She is working in a clinic every Monday.'
exercises.answer: She works in a clinic every Monday.
exercises.hint: 'Every Monday' = routine. Which tense is correct for routines?
exercises.explanation: For regular routines and habits, use present simple — not present continuous. 'Every Monday' is a clear signal for present simple.

exercises.question: Find and fix the mistake: 'She always is late to meetings.'
exercises.answer: She is always late to meetings.
exercises.hint: Where does a frequency adverb go when the verb is 'be'?
exercises.explanation: With the verb 'be', frequency adverbs go AFTER the verb. She IS always late — not she always IS late.

exercises.question: Put the words in the correct order:
exercises.answer: She does not exercise every morning.
exercises.hint: Subject first. 'does not' comes before the base verb. Time expression at the end.
exercises.explanation: Negative: subject + does not + base verb + time expression. 'Does not' = 'doesn't' in informal English.

exercises.question: Put the words in the correct order:
exercises.hint: Question word comes first, then do, then subject, then verb
exercises.explanation: Wh-question: question word + do/does + subject + base verb. Standard English question word order.

exercises.question: Put the words in the correct order:
exercises.answer: The bus comes twice an hour.
exercises.hint: The bus = it → verb gets -s. Frequency phrase at the end.
exercises.explanation: 'The bus' = it, so come → comes. 'Twice an hour' is a frequency expression that goes at the end.

exercises.question: Put the words in the correct order:
exercises.hint: Does comes first in yes/no questions. Subject goes between does and the verb.
exercises.explanation: Yes/no question with does: Does + subject + base verb? Your manager = he/she → Does.

exercises.question: Put the words in the correct order:
exercises.answer: They usually open the office at eight.
exercises.hint: Frequency adverbs like 'usually' go between the subject and the main verb.
exercises.explanation: Frequency adverbs go before the main verb: subject + usually + verb + object + time.

speak.sentence: I usually wake up at seven o'clock in the morning.
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Usually' = YOO-zhoo-uh-lee — four syllables. 'Wake' — the vowel sounds like the 'a' in 'cake', not the 'a' in 'cat'. Don't swallow the final -k.

speak.sentence: Do you speak any other languages besides Hebrew?
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Languages' = LANG-gwij-iz — three syllables. Don't say 'languagEES'. 'Besides' = bih-SYDZ — stress on the second syllable.

speak.sentence: She doesn't work on Fridays because she studies in the afternoon.
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Doesn't' = DUZ-unt — say it as one smooth word. 'Studies' = STUD-eez — two syllables. 'Because' = bih-KOZ — stress on second syllable.

speak.sentence: My brother works as an engineer and travels a lot for his job.
chunks: My brother works as an engineer
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Engineer' = en-jih-NEER — three syllables, stress on the last. 'Travels' = TRAV-ulz. Don't forget the final -s sound on both 'works' and 'travels'.

speak.sentence: How often do you exercise during the week?
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Often' — the T is silent: OFF-en. 'Exercise' = EK-ser-syz — three syllables, stress on the first. 'During' = DYOOR-ing.

speak.sentence: The last bus to the city centre leaves at half past eleven.
chunks: The last bus to the city centre
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Centre' = SEN-tuh. 'Leaves' — make sure you pronounce the final -z sound. 'Eleven' = ih-LEV-en — stress on the second syllable.

speak.sentence: I don't think it's a good idea to leave so late at night.
chunks: to leave so late at night.
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Think' — the 'th' sound: put your tongue lightly between your teeth and blow air. Don't say 'tink'. 'Idea' = eye-DEE-uh — three syllables.

speak.sentence: Does your company offer any work-from-home options these days?
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Company' = KUM-puh-nee — three syllables, stress on the first. 'Offer' = OFF-er. 'Options' = OP-shunz. 'These' — th sound, not 'd' sound.

speak.sentence: My parents always drink tea together after dinner.
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Parents' = PAIR-unts — stress on first syllable. 'Always' = AWL-wayz. 'Together' = tuh-GETH-er — stress on second syllable. Practice the -th- in together.

speak.sentence: She studies hard but she never worries about exams.
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Studies' = STUD-eez. 'Never' = NEV-er — stress on first syllable. 'Worries' = WUR-eez. 'Exams' = ig-ZAMZ — stress on second syllable.
